title: Sudden pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness in a young stimulant user

chart.cc: Sudden chest pain and shortness of breath
chart.oneLiner: 22M, tall and thin with asthma on chronic steroids and a history of stimulant/cocaine use, presents with the abrupt onset of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness shortly after a night of vaping and inhaled cocaine.

opening: My right side started hurting really sharply out of nowhere and now I can't seem to catch my breath — and honestly my heart is pounding too.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 22-year-old man, tall and thin with long arms and long fingers (Marfanoid habitus), mildly anxious, breathing fast and a little jittery, holding his right chest. Cooperative but sheepish about last night's drug use.

HPI: Abrupt onset about 2 hours ago, at rest, of sharp RIGHT-sided chest pain that is worse with breathing (pleuritic) and a sensation of breathlessness. The pain came on a few hours after a night out where he was vaping heavily and snorted/inhaled some cocaine (only volunteers the drug use if asked specifically about substances/drugs). No blunt trauma, no heavy lifting. Pain does NOT radiate to the arm/jaw and is not crushing/pressure-like, though his heart has been racing and pounding since the cocaine. NO fever, NO productive cough or purulent sputum (a little dry cough only), NO leg swelling or calf pain, NO recent long travel or immobilization. He has had one similar but milder episode of sudden chest pain last year that resolved on its own (only if asked about prior episodes). Smokes about half a pack a day and vapes high-THC oil daily (only if asked about tobacco/vaping). Uses cocaine recreationally a few times a month, last night by insufflation/inhalation (only if asked about recreational drugs). Family history: father had a 'collapsed lung' in his twenties and an aunt has Marfan syndrome (only if asked about family history).
PMH: mild persistent ASTHMA (a few ED visits, never intubated); tall/marfanoid habitus; anxiety/ADHD. He is mid-way through a short course of ORAL PREDNISONE for an asthma flare this week and uses his albuterol inhaler many times a day lately. MEDS: fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler daily; albuterol — using it very frequently this week; prednisone 40 mg daily (day 3 of a 5-day burst); lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) for ADHD each morning; occasional ibuprofen. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: tobacco half pack/day, daily high-THC vaping, recreational cocaine a few times monthly (last night), social alcohol. FAMILY: father with spontaneous 'collapsed lung' in youth; maternal aunt with Marfan syndrome (if asked).
ROS: dry cough, palpitations and the racing heart since cocaine; no fever, no hemoptysis, no leg swelling/calf pain, no syncope.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tall, thin, long-limbed young man with a high-arched palate and long fingers (arm span exceeds height), mild respiratory distress, jittery, speaking in short full sentences. Trachea MIDLINE (no deviation — this is NOT a tension pneumothorax). No subcutaneous crepitus over the neck/chest, no Hamman's crunch (argues against pneumomediastinum).
- Vitals: BP 142/86, HR 118, RR 26, 37.1°C, SpO2 90% RA.
- Lungs: DECREASED/absent breath sounds over the RIGHT upper hemithorax, HYPERRESONANT to percussion on the right, DECREASED tactile fremitus on the right. Left lung: scattered faint expiratory wheeze (his asthma), otherwise clear with present breath sounds.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur, no rub, no JVD; PMI not displaced. Skin warm and slightly diaphoretic. Extremities: long fingers, no edema, no calf tenderness. No chest-wall tenderness to palpation, no crepitus.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the cocaine, vaping, tobacco, prednisone burst, frequent albuterol, stimulant (Vyvanse), the prior episode, the Marfan family history, or the asthma unless asked about substances/drugs, tobacco/vaping, medications, prior episodes, family history, or past medical history respectively.
- Hyperresonance, decreased breath sounds, decreased fremitus on the right, and the left-sided asthmatic wheeze are revealed ONLY if the corresponding exam is performed.
- Trachea is MIDLINE, there is NO subcutaneous crepitus and NO Hamman's crunch, and the patient is normotensive-to-hypertensive (not hypotensive); if asked, there is no tracheal deviation and no hypotension (rules against tension physiology and against pneumomediastinum).
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 13.8 (mild leukocytosis, attributable to the corticosteroid burst and catecholamine demargination rather than infection — no bandemia), Hgb 15.1, Plt 268.
labs.BMP: Na 139, K 3.1 (HYPOKALEMIA — driven by frequent beta-agonist use, the cocaine/stimulant catecholamine surge, and steroids), HCO3 24, BUN 16, Cr 0.9, glucose 138 (mild hyperglycemia on prednisone). Anion gap = 139 − (103 + 24) = 12 (normal).
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin 14 ng/L then 15 ng/L on repeat — at the upper reference limit and flat (no rise/fall), arguing against acute MI despite the cocaine exposure.
labs.D-dimer: Mildly elevated at 0.7 µg/mL FEU (nonspecific; can rise with any acute cardiopulmonary stress and does not by itself diagnose PE — interpret with pretest probability).
labs.Lactate: 2.4 mmol/L (mildly elevated — consistent with a catecholamine/beta-agonist surge and the work of breathing, NOT necessarily tissue hypoperfusion).
labs.ABG: pH 7.49, PaCO2 31, PaO2 66 on room air, A-a gradient widened — hypoxemia with a respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation; hypoxemia out of proportion to a simple anxiety attack.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~118 bpm with flattened T waves and prominent U waves and a long QU/borderline-prolonged QTc — a hypokalaemia pattern — without acute ischemic ST-elevation; this is the patient's OWN tracing and reflects the K of 3.1 plus stimulant/beta-agonist effect.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with flattened T waves, prominent U waves, and a long QU interval (hypokalaemia), no acute ST-elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with flattened T waves, U waves, and a long QU interval consistent with hypokalaemia
searchTerms: flattened T wave prominent U wave long QU sinus tachycardia 12 lead
ekg.clinicalVignette: A tall thin young man with frequent beta-agonist use, a steroid burst, and overnight cocaine presents with pleuritic chest pain, palpitations, and a serum potassium of 3.1.
ekg.clinicalNote: Integrated read: this is the patient's own EKG. The grading findings live on the ekg_read step (hypokalaemia pattern with U waves and borderline-long QTc, no STEMI), and the hypokalaemia/QTc must be carried into the management plan (replete K+/Mg before it precipitates a malignant arrhythmia, especially with a stimulant on board).

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Right-sided pneumothorax: visible visceral pleural line with absent lung markings peripheral to it in the right upper hemithorax, partial lung collapse, and NO mediastinal shift (no tension); no pneumomediastinum (no lucent stripe along the mediastinum), no consolidation.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings, and no mediastinal shift. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph showing a moderate-to-large right-sided pneumothorax with visible pleural line and no mediastinal shift
searchTerms: visceral pleural line lung markings absent radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: A tall thin young man presents with abrupt right-sided pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical in this young stimulant user with sudden pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis). Explicitly NAME THREE mechanisms/etiologies by which his drug and medication history could produce acute chest pain and breathlessness.
steps.rubric: Spontaneous pneumothorax — leading; primary in a tall marfanoid patient, possibly secondary given asthma/inhaled-drug barotrauma (leading,7; accepts primary OR secondary spontaneous pneumothorax). Cocaine-associated chest pain / ACS-coronary vasospasm — genuine competitor given last-night cocaine, hypertension, tachycardia, palpitations (CANT-MISS,5). Tension pneumothorax to actively exclude (CANT-MISS,3). Pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS,2). Pneumomediastinum from forced inhalation/Valsalva/barotrauma (core,2). Names three drug/med MECHANISMS — e.g. cocaine→coronary vasospasm/sympathetic surge, barotrauma/alveolar rupture from inhalational drug use & cough, beta-agonist/stimulant→tachyarrhythmia & hypokalemia, connective-tissue/steroid-related apical bleb rupture (core,3). Named leading dx with justification + imperative to assess tension physiology (tracheal deviation, hypotension) (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on pneumothorax and ignoring the cocaine/ACS risk (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Spontaneous pneumothorax (right-sided, in a tall marfanoid smoker — primary, though his asthma and inhalational drug use make a secondary/barotraumatic pneumothorax plausible). Genuine competing diagnosis: cocaine-associated chest pain with possible ACS from coronary vasospasm/sympathetic surge (last-night cocaine, hypertension, tachycardia, palpitations) — this fork must be resolved, not dismissed. Must-not-miss: Tension pneumothorax (actively exclude) and Pulmonary embolism. Also consider pneumomediastinum from forced inhalation/Valsalva. Three drug/medication MECHANISMS of his chest pain and dyspnea: (1) cocaine-induced coronary vasospasm and catecholamine surge → ischemia/arrhythmia; (2) barotrauma/alveolar rupture from forceful inhalation, breath-holding, and coughing during vaping/insufflation → pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum; (3) frequent beta-agonist plus stimulant (lisdexamfetamine) and the steroid burst → tachyarrhythmia and hypokalemia. Connective-tissue/bleb rupture (Marfanoid habitus, family history) is a fourth predisposing mechanism.

coreActions.item: Names three drug/medication mechanisms of chest pain/dyspnea
Named leading dx with justification + assesses tension physiology

penalties.item: Anchors on pneumothorax and ignores the cocaine/ACS risk

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? (Consider what changes management, what excludes the must-not-miss diagnoses, and what his drug/medication history mandates.)
steps.rubric: Upright chest X-ray (CANT-MISS,4). 12-lead EKG to evaluate for cocaine-related ischemia AND drug/electrolyte effect (CANT-MISS,3). BMP/electrolytes given heavy beta-agonist + stimulant + steroid use (3). Serial high-sensitivity troponin given cocaine exposure (2). Continuous pulse oximetry / telemetry & repeat vitals to monitor for deterioration and arrhythmia (2). ABG if hypoxemic or not improving (1). Risk-stratify for PE (D-dimer/Wells) (1). CBC; lactate (1). PENALTY: ordering CT angiogram or extensive cardiac imaging before a simple confirmatory chest X-ray in a classic young pneumothorax (-2); delaying needle decompression to obtain imaging IF the patient becomes hemodynamically unstable/tension is suspected (-3); giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain (unopposed alpha) (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: upright CXR (confirms the pneumothorax and excludes pneumomediastinum/tension); 12-lead EKG (cocaine ischemia AND drug/electrolyte effect); BMP/electrolytes (expect hypokalemia from beta-agonist/stimulant/steroid); serial high-sensitivity troponin (cocaine exposure); continuous pulse oximetry/telemetry and repeat vitals; ABG if hypoxemic or not improving; risk-stratify for PE with D-dimer/Wells; CBC; lactate. Avoid a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine-related chest pain (unopposed alpha vasoconstriction).

coreActions.item: Continuous pulse oximetry / telemetry and repeat vitals
ABG if hypoxemic or not improving

penalties.item: Ordering CT/extensive cardiac imaging before a simple confirmatory chest X-ray in a classic young pneumothorax
Delaying needle decompression to obtain imaging if tension physiology is suspected
Giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain (unopposed alpha)

optionalTests: Continuous pulse oximetry / telemetry and repeat vitals
ABG if hypoxemic or not improving
contraindicatedTests: Ordering CT/extensive cardiac imaging before a simple confirmatory chest X-ray in a classic young pneumothorax
Giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain (unopposed alpha)
orderSensitiveActions: Delaying needle decompression to obtain imaging if tension physiology is suspected

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state what they imply for his immediate management.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia (~118 bpm) with a HYPOKALAEMIA pattern: flattened/low T waves, prominent U waves, and a long QU/borderline-prolonged QTc; NO acute ischemic ST-elevation despite the cocaine. CREDIT: rate & rhythm — sinus tachycardia (4); recognizes the hypokalaemia pattern (flat T/U waves/long QU) and links it to the K of 3.1 and his beta-agonist/stimulant/steroid use (6); states there is NO acute STEMI/ischemic injury pattern (3); systematic read & comments the long QTc raises arrhythmia risk needing K+/Mg repletion (2). PENALTY: over-calling acute ischemia/STEMI that is not present, or missing the hypokalaemia/QTc and its arrhythmia implication (-3).
keyFindings: hypokalaemia pattern: flattened T waves, prominent U waves, long QU / borderline-prolonged QTc
no acute ischemic ST-elevation despite cocaine
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a hypokalaemia pattern (flat T waves, U waves, long QU / borderline-long QTc) and no acute STEMI — repletion of K+/Mg is needed given the prolonged QTc and stimulant on board.
commonMistakes: over-calling acute ischemia / STEMI from the cocaine context when the ST segments are not elevated
missing the U waves / long QU and failing to connect them to the hypokalaemia and arrhythmia risk
calling the tracing normal sinus tachycardia and ignoring the electrolyte pattern
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~118 bpm. There is a hypokalaemia pattern — flattened/low T waves, prominent U waves, and a long QU interval / borderline-prolonged QTc — fitting his serum potassium of 3.1 from heavy beta-agonist use, the stimulant (lisdexamfetamine), and the steroid burst. Importantly there is NO acute ST-elevation or ischemic injury pattern despite the cocaine exposure, which argues against an evolving STEMI (correlate with serial troponin). The prolonged QTc plus a stimulant and ongoing beta-agonist puts him at risk for a malignant ventricular arrhythmia, so this EKG mandates potassium and magnesium repletion and telemetry as part of management.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with a hypokalaemia pattern (flat T waves, U waves, long QU / borderline-long QTc) and no acute STEMI — repletion of K+/Mg is needed given the prolonged QTc and stimulant on board.
coreActions: hypokalaemia pattern: flattened T waves, prominent U waves, long QU / borderline-prolonged QTc
no acute ischemic ST-elevation despite cocaine

penalties.label: over-calls acute ischemia/STEMI, or misses the hypokalaemia/QTc and its arrhythmia implication

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = right pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line and absent peripheral lung markings; NO mediastinal shift (no tension); NO pneumomediastinal lucent stripe. CREDIT: identifies the pneumothorax / visceral pleural line (5); explicitly comments on the absence of mediastinal shift (rules out tension) (3); comments there is no pneumomediastinum despite the inhalational drug use (2). PENALTY: missing the pneumothorax / calling the film normal (-3).
keyFindings: identifies a visible visceral pleural line with absent peripheral lung markings (right pneumothorax)
notes there is no mediastinal/tracheal shift (no tension)
no pneumomediastinal lucent stripe despite inhalational drug use
criticalFindings: Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings) with no mediastinal shift and no pneumomediastinum.
commonMistakes: missing a subtle apical pneumothorax / calling the film normal
mistaking a skin fold or bulla for the pleural line
failing to comment on the pertinent negative of pneumomediastinum
steps.idealAnswer: Right-sided pneumothorax: visible visceral pleural line with absent lung markings peripherally and partial lung collapse. No mediastinal shift, so no tension physiology, and no lucent stripe along the mediastinum to suggest pneumomediastinum despite the inhalational drug use. Lungs otherwise without consolidation.

criticalActions: Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings) with no mediastinal shift and no pneumomediastinum.
coreActions: identifies a visible visceral pleural line with absent peripheral lung markings (right pneumothorax)
notes there is no mediastinal/tracheal shift (no tension)
no pneumomediastinal lucent stripe despite inhalational drug use

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The chest X-ray shows a right-sided pneumothorax with a visceral pleural line and no mediastinal shift; the EKG shows sinus tachycardia with a hypokalaemia pattern (U waves, long QU) and no STEMI; serial troponins are flat at the upper reference limit; K is 3.1; the patient remains stable with a midline trachea. Commit to your single most likely diagnosis, name the discriminating tests, state how you resolved the cocaine/ACS competitor, and acknowledge any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Commits to (primary or secondary) spontaneous pneumothorax as the unifying cause of the pleuritic pain and hypoxia (6). Resolves the cocaine/ACS competitor — flat serial troponins and no STEMI on the patient's own EKG argue against acute MI, though cocaine chest pain still warrants observation; explicitly excludes tension (stable vitals, midline trachea, no shift) and addresses PE (low pretest probability; a mildly elevated D-dimer is nonspecific). Identifies the concurrent hypokalaemia with QTc prolongation as a SEPARATE active problem to treat, and names residual uncertainty (e.g. serial troponin/observation period for the cocaine exposure) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Single most likely diagnosis: spontaneous pneumothorax (right-sided) — primary given his tall marfanoid habitus and family history, though his asthma and forceful inhalational drug use make a secondary/barotraumatic pneumothorax plausible; it unifies the pleuritic pain, hypoxia, and the lateralizing exam. Discriminating test: the upright CXR (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral markings), with the midline trachea and absence of mediastinal shift excluding tension. Resolving the cocaine/ACS competitor: his own EKG shows no STEMI and serial high-sensitivity troponins are flat at the upper reference limit, arguing strongly against an acute MI — but because cocaine chest pain can still cause delayed ischemia, he warrants a period of monitored observation with serial troponins rather than immediate clearance. PE is unlikely (low pretest probability; the mildly elevated D-dimer is nonspecific and explained by acute cardiopulmonary stress). Importantly, the hypokalaemia (K 3.1) with U waves and a borderline-long QTc is a SEPARATE concurrent problem — driven by beta-agonist, stimulant, and steroid use — that must be corrected to prevent arrhythmia. Residual uncertainty: completing the troponin/observation window for the cocaine exposure.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Include how you treat the EKG/electrolyte finding, how you handle the cocaine exposure, and your DISPOSITION decision — specifically state when this patient could be SAFELY discharged versus admitted, and what counseling/follow-up that requires.
steps.rubric: Per MGH pneumothorax management: size/symptom-based intervention — needle aspiration or small-bore chest tube/pigtail for this large/symptomatic pneumothorax vs observation only for a small asymptomatic one (CANT-MISS,5); supplemental high-flow oxygen to speed pleural reabsorption (CANT-MISS,4); CORRECT the hypokalaemia — replete potassium AND magnesium given the U waves/long QTc and stimulant on board, on telemetry (CANT-MISS,3); benzodiazepines as first-line for cocaine-related chest pain/hypertension and AVOID non-selective beta-blockers (unopposed alpha) (2); analgesia (2); SAFE-DISCHARGE/DISPOSITION decision — admit/observe a symptomatic large pneumothorax and a cocaine-exposed patient on telemetry with serial CXR and troponins; he is dischargeable only once the lung is re-expanded/stable on repeat imaging, troponins have ruled out, K/QTc are corrected, and with strict return precautions, smoking/vaping/cocaine-cessation counseling, and thoracic-surgery follow-up (core,3); smoking/vaping & cocaine cessation counseling (2); thoracic surgery consult & pleurodesis/VATS discussion for recurrence or persistent air leak (1); immediate needle decompression THEN chest tube if tension develops (bonus,1). PENALTY: discharging this large symptomatic pneumothorax (or an unmonitored cocaine-exposed patient with a long QTc) without intervention, repletion, or follow-up (-4); giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain (-3); failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Spontaneous pneumothorax, large and symptomatic: place a small-bore chest tube/pigtail catheter (or perform needle aspiration first per size/symptoms; observation alone is only for a small asymptomatic pneumothorax). Give high-flow supplemental oxygen to hasten pleural air reabsorption. Treat the EKG/electrolyte finding: replete POTASSIUM and MAGNESIUM and keep him on telemetry, because the hypokalaemia with U waves and a borderline-long QTc — plus the stimulant and beta-agonist — predisposes to arrhythmia. Handle the cocaine exposure with benzodiazepines as first-line for chest pain/hypertension/tachycardia and AVOID non-selective beta-blockers (unopposed alpha vasoconstriction); rule out MI with serial troponins. Provide analgesia. DISPOSITION / SAFE DISCHARGE: admit and observe — a symptomatic large pneumothorax with a chest tube plus a cocaine-exposed patient with a prolonged QTc both warrant monitored inpatient care with serial CXR and troponins. He becomes safe for discharge only once the lung is re-expanded and stable on repeat imaging (and any tube removed/air leak resolved), serial troponins have excluded MI, and the potassium/QTc are corrected — discharged with strict return precautions, smoking/vaping AND cocaine-cessation counseling, and thoracic surgery follow-up. Consult thoracic surgery and discuss pleurodesis/VATS for recurrence or a persistent air leak. If tension physiology develops at any point, perform immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube.

criticalActions.item: Size/symptom-based intervention (aspiration/chest tube for this large symptomatic pneumothorax vs observation for a small one)
Replete potassium AND magnesium for hypokalaemia/long QTc on telemetry

coreActions.item: Benzodiazepines first-line for cocaine chest pain; avoid non-selective beta-blockers
Safe-discharge/disposition decision: admit on telemetry, dischargeable only once lung re-expanded/stable, troponins ruled out, K/QTc corrected, with return precautions and follow-up
Thoracic surgery consult / pleurodesis discussion for recurrence

bonusActions.item: Immediate needle decompression then chest tube if tension develops

penalties.item: Discharging this large symptomatic pneumothorax or an unmonitored cocaine-exposed patient with a long QTc without intervention, repletion, or follow-up
Giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain
Failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging

requiredActions: Size/symptom-based intervention (aspiration/chest tube for this large symptomatic pneumothorax vs observation for a small one)
Replete potassium AND magnesium for hypokalaemia/long QTc on telemetry
optionalActions: Benzodiazepines first-line for cocaine chest pain; avoid non-selective beta-blockers
Safe-discharge/disposition decision: admit on telemetry, dischargeable only once lung re-expanded/stable, troponins ruled out, K/QTc corrected, with return precautions and follow-up
Thoracic surgery consult / pleurodesis discussion for recurrence
Immediate needle decompression then chest tube if tension develops
unsafeActions: Discharging this large symptomatic pneumothorax or an unmonitored cocaine-exposed patient with a long QTc without intervention, repletion, or follow-up
Giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain
Failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging
steps.disposition: Admit on telemetry with chest tube; safe discharge only once lung re-expanded/stable on repeat CXR, troponins ruled out, and K/QTc corrected, with cessation counseling and thoracic-surgery follow-up; immediate decompression and ICU if tension physiology develops

physicalExamMappings.finding: Lungs: DECREASED/absent breath sounds over the RIGHT upper hemithorax. Left lung: scattered faint expiratory wheeze (asthma), otherwise breath sounds present.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Breath sounds normal and symmetric bilaterally.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Lungs: HYPERRESONANT to percussion over the right hemithorax with DECREASED tactile fremitus on the right.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Resonant to percussion with symmetric tactile fremitus.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Chest wall: no tenderness, no subcutaneous crepitus over the neck/chest (argues against pneumomediastinum).

General: tall, thin, long-limbed young man with a high-arched palate and long fingers (Marfanoid), in mild respiratory distress, jittery. Trachea MIDLINE (no deviation — NOT a tension pneumothorax); no Hamman's crunch.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur, no rub, no JVD; PMI not displaced; skin warm and slightly diaphoretic.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 22M, tall and marfanoid, with asthma on a steroid burst and frequent beta-agonist use, a stimulant (lisdexamfetamine), and overnight cocaine, presents with the abrupt onset of right-sided pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, palpitations, and hypertension. The diagnosis is Spontaneous pneumothorax (primary vs secondary/barotraumatic), reached by eliciting the focused history (including the sensitive drug/medication history), performing the targeted exam, and interpreting a directed workup while resolving the genuine cocaine/ACS competitor and recognizing a concurrent hypokalaemia with QTc prolongation as a separate problem to treat.
keyHistoryClues: sudden pleuritic pain at rest after vaping and inhaled cocaine
tall marfanoid habitus with family history of collapsed lung and Marfan
heavy beta-agonist use plus a steroid burst and a stimulant
palpitations and a racing heart since the cocaine
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Lungs: DECREASED/absent breath sounds over the RIGHT upper hemithorax. Left lung: scattered faint expiratory wheeze (asthma), otherwise breath sounds present.
Lungs: HYPERRESONANT to percussion over the right hemithorax with DECREASED tactile fremitus on the right.
Chest wall: no tenderness, no subcutaneous crepitus over the neck/chest (argues against pneumomediastinum).

General: tall, thin, long-limbed young man with a high-arched palate and long fingers (Marfanoid), in mild respiratory distress, jittery. Trachea MIDLINE (no deviation — NOT a tension pneumothorax); no Hamman's crunch.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (tachycardia, hypertension, hypoxia in a jittery young man).
Elicit the focused history, including the sensitive drug and medication history (cocaine, vaping, stimulant, steroid burst, frequent albuterol) only on direct, nonjudgmental questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal the lateralizing pneumothorax findings while excluding tension and pneumomediastinum.
Build a differential led by Spontaneous pneumothorax with a genuine cocaine/ACS competitor, naming three drug/medication mechanisms of chest pain and dyspnea.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup (CXR + EKG + electrolytes + serial troponin) and interpret the patient's own EKG (hypokalaemia/QTc) and CXR.

Commit to Spontaneous pneumothorax, treat the concurrent hypokalaemia/QTc, manage the cocaine exposure correctly, and make a defensible admit-versus-safe-discharge disposition with counseling and follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on pneumothorax and ignoring the cocaine/ACS risk
Over-calling a STEMI on the patient's own EKG or missing the hypokalaemia/QTc
Giving a non-selective beta-blocker for cocaine chest pain (unopposed alpha)
Discharging a large symptomatic pneumothorax or an unmonitored long-QTc patient without repletion or follow-up
Failing to decompress a tension pneumothorax while awaiting imaging
managementPearls: Size/symptom-based intervention (aspiration/chest tube for a large symptomatic pneumothorax vs observation for a small one)
Replete potassium AND magnesium for hypokalaemia/long QTc on telemetry
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with a hypokalaemia pattern (U waves, long QU / borderline-long QTc) and no acute STEMI.
Right-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line and absent peripheral lung markings; no mediastinal shift (no tension) and no pneumomediastinum.
criticalManagementItems: Replete potassium AND magnesium for hypokalaemia/long QTc on telemetry
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a hypokalaemia pattern (U waves, long QU) and no acute STEMI.

criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a hypokalaemia pattern (U waves, long QU / borderline-long QTc) and no acute STEMI.
Right-sided pneumothorax (visceral pleural line, absent peripheral lung markings) with no mediastinal shift and no pneumomediastinum.
imageInterpretationPearls: An integrated tracing is the patient's OWN study — read the rhythm, then connect the repolarization changes to the bedside electrolytes and drugs.
Cocaine context does not equal STEMI — if the ST segments are not elevated and serial troponins are flat, do not invent ischemia.
On the chest film, the deliberate pertinent negatives are the absence of mediastinal shift (no tension) and the absence of a pneumomediastinal stripe.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission and is nonjudgmental before sensitive drug-use questions
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
